1:
"And it came to pass, when men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born unto them," The brief verse notes the multiplying of mankind and the birth of daughters. The grammatical voices note the increase of the race. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born unto them" — mankind multiplied rapidly (the long-lived antediluvians, ch. 5, peopling the earth); daughters were born among the increasing population; the verse sets the scene for the intermarriages (v.2) that led to the universal corruption (v.5) and the flood.

On "men began to multiply on the face of the earth," the witnesses note the population. Ellicott and Gill note that the race had greatly increased since Adam; the earth was filling with people; the multiplication of mankind, blessed by God (1:28), now becomes the occasion of widespread wickedness as the line of Seth and the line of Cain mingle.

On "daughters were born unto them," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the daughters: "daughters were born unto them" — the birth of daughters is noted because their beauty (v.2) drew the sons of God; the verse prepares for the corrupting intermarriages; the increase of the race and the birth of fair daughters set the stage for the apostasy and corruption that brought on the flood. The verse displays the multiplying of mankind and the birth of daughters.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the multiplying — "men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born unto them," the rapid increase of the race and the birth of daughters (Keil, Gill); the population filling the earth, the line of Seth and Cain mingling (Ellicott, Gill); "daughters were born," the fair daughters who drew the sons of God (v.2, Henry). The verse displays the increasing race. The verse marks the multiplying: of men. The verse displays the daughters: born. The verse displays the scene: set for v.2. The verse displays the occasion: of corruption. The increase displays the race multiplying toward the corruption that brought the flood. The verse displays the men: multiplying. The verse displays the daughters: born. The verse displays the stage: set. The multiplying of mankind and the birth of daughters display the setting for the apostasy: when men multiplied and daughters were born, the scene was set for the corrupting intermarriages (v.2) that led to the wickedness and the flood. The verse displays the multiplying race, in the sons of God's intermarriage (v.2).
2:
"That the sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were fair; and they took them wives of all which they chose." The witnesses note the sons of God taking wives of the daughters of men — the crux of their identity. On "the sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were fair," interpreters divide over the "sons of God." The reformed Calvin and Gill (the dominant Protestant view) hold the "sons of God" are the godly line of Seth (the worshippers of God, 4:26), and the "daughters of men" the ungodly line of Cain; the apostasy lay in the godly intermarrying with the ungodly, choosing wives by mere beauty, mingling the holy seed with the profane, and so corrupting the church. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and the ancient/Enochic tradition (with 1 Peter 3:19-20; 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 6) hold the "sons of God" are angels (fallen angels), who took human wives — the angelic interpretation; Gill and Clarke note this view but object (Christ says the angels neither marry nor are given in marriage, Matthew 22:30). A third view (the Jewish, and some) takes the "sons of God" as the nobles, great men, or dynastic rulers (the powerful, the kings), and the "daughters of men" as women of lower rank.

On "they took them wives of all which they chose," the witnesses note the corrupt choice. Calvin and Ellicott note that they "took them wives of all which they chose" — choosing wives by beauty and lust, without regard to godliness; the unrestrained, self-willed choice; whether the godly choosing the ungodly, the angels the human, or the great men taking many wives (polygamy and tyranny); the corruption of marriage and the mingling that brought on the universal wickedness. The verse displays the sons of God taking wives of the daughters of men.

The witnesses agree the substance: the "sons of God" saw and took the "daughters of men" as wives by their beauty, a corrupting union that led to the wickedness and the flood (Calvin, Gill, Keil, Ellicott). The identity bears the great crux: the godly line of Seth intermarrying with the Cainites (the dominant Protestant view — Calvin, Gill) vs fallen angels taking human wives (the angelic view — Keil; 1 Peter 3:19; 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 6) vs the nobles/dynastic rulers (the Jewish reading). The verse displays the sons of God crux. The verse marks the sons of God: who they are. The verse displays the debate: Sethites, angels, or nobles. The verse displays the daughters: of men. The verse displays the choice: by beauty. The union displays the corrupting intermarriage that brought the flood. The verse displays the sons: of God. The verse displays the wives: chosen. The verse displays the corruption: begun. The sons of God taking wives of the daughters of men display the great crux of the chapter: the sons of God took wives of the daughters of men by their beauty — read by the dominant Protestant view as the godly Sethites intermarrying with the ungodly Cainites (Calvin, Gill), by the angelic view as fallen angels taking human wives (Keil; Jude 6), and by the Jewish view as the nobles/great men. The verse displays the corrupting union, with God's word of judgment (v.3).
3:
"And the LORD said, My spirit shall not always strive with man, for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years." The witnesses note God's word that his Spirit shall not always strive, and the hundred and twenty years — the crux of the period. On "My spirit shall not always strive with man," interpreters note the striving. The puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian Clarke read "strive" as the Spirit's contending with man — by conscience, by the preaching of Noah (a preacher of righteousness, 2 Peter 2:5), and by God's patience; God's Spirit strives with the sinner, but will not strive forever; if man persists in sin, the Spirit will cease to strive, and judgment will come. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the alternative rendering "abide" or "dwell" — "my spirit shall not always abide (remain) in man" (the breath/life of God not remaining in man forever); though "strive" (contend) is well supported; the two renderings differ on whether the Spirit contends with man or the life-breath remains in him.

On "for that he also is flesh," the witnesses note the reason. Calvin and Gill note that "for that he also is flesh" — man is fallen, carnal, given over to fleshly corruption; his being "flesh" (sinful, corrupt) is why the Spirit will not always strive; the carnality of man provokes the withdrawal of the Spirit.

On "his days shall be an hundred and twenty years," interpreters divide. The puritan Henry and reformed Gill read the 120 years as a respite, a reprieve, a space of repentance before the flood — God's longsuffering granting 120 years' warning (during which Noah built the ark and preached), that men might repent; the period of probation. A second reading takes the 120 years as the new limit on human lifespan — that henceforth man's life would be shortened to 120 years. The verse displays God's word that his Spirit shall not always strive.

The witnesses agree the substance: God declares that his Spirit will not always strive with sinful man, and appoints a hundred and twenty years (Henry, Clarke, Calvin, Gill, Ellicott). Two cruxes: "strive" (the Spirit contending with man, by conscience and Noah's preaching — Henry, Clarke) vs "abide/remain" (the breath of life not remaining — Ellicott); and the 120 years = a respite/space of repentance before the flood (the dominant reading — Henry, Gill) vs the shortening of human lifespan to 120 years. The verse displays the 120-years crux. The verse marks the Spirit: not always striving. The verse displays the debate: strive or abide; respite or lifespan. The verse displays the flesh: man's carnality. The verse displays the years: a hundred and twenty. The word displays God's patience bounded and the term appointed. The verse displays the Spirit: striving. The verse displays the years: 120. The verse displays the respite: granted. God's word that his Spirit shall not always strive, and the hundred and twenty years, display God's bounded patience and a crux: the Spirit will not always strive with carnal man, and his days shall be a hundred and twenty years — read as a respite of probation before the flood (Henry, Gill) or the new limit on human life, the "strive" being the Spirit's contending or the breath's abiding. The verse displays the appointed term, the giants of those days (v.4).
4:
"There were giants in the earth in those days; and also after that, when the sons of God came in unto the daughters of men, and they bare children to them, the same became mighty men which were of old, men of renown." The witnesses note the giants and mighty men of old. On "There were giants in the earth in those days," the grammatical voices note the Nephilim. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "there were giants (Nephilim) in the earth in those days" — the "Nephilim" (variously rendered "giants," or "fallen ones," or "violent ones") were in the earth in those days; men of great stature, strength, or violence; whether literal giants or mighty, violent men; their presence marks the age of corruption.

On "when the sons of God came in unto the daughters of men, and they bare children to them," the witnesses note the offspring. Ellicott and Gill note that the offspring of the union of the sons of God and the daughters of men (v.2) "became mighty men" — on the angelic view, the Nephilim are the offspring of angels and women; on the Sethite view, the mighty men are the offspring of the mixed marriages; the union produced a race of powerful, renowned men.

On "the same became mighty men which were of old, men of renown," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note their fame: "mighty men which were of old, men of renown (men of name)" — famous, powerful men, renowned for their strength, exploits, or (more likely) their violence and tyranny; "men of renown" not for virtue but for might and infamy; the heroes of the corrupt age, whose violence filled the earth (v.11). The verse displays the giants and mighty men of old.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Nephilim — "there were giants (Nephilim) in the earth in those days," men of great stature, strength, or violence (Keil, Ellicott); the offspring of the union (v.2) becoming mighty men (Ellicott, Gill); "mighty men... men of renown," famous, powerful men, renowned for might and violence (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mighty men. The verse marks the giants: Nephilim. The verse displays the offspring: of the union. The verse displays the might: of the men. The verse displays the renown: of old. The age displays the giants and mighty men filling the earth with violence. The verse displays the Nephilim: present. The verse displays the mighty: men. The verse displays the renown: infamous. The giants and mighty men of old display the heroes of the corrupt age: there were Nephilim in those days, and the offspring of the sons of God and the daughters of men became mighty men of renown — powerful, famous men, whose might and violence marked the age that brought on the flood. The verse displays the mighty men, before God's view of man's wickedness (v.5).
5:
"And God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually." The witnesses note the total corruption of man's heart. On "God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth," the grammatical voices note the universal wickedness. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth" — God beheld the vast, universal wickedness of mankind; the corruption had become enormous and widespread; the whole race, save Noah, was sunk in sin; God's all-seeing eye marks the depth of human depravity before the flood.

On "every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the depth of depravity. Calvin and Gill note the searching description: "every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually" — the very imaginations and thoughts of man's heart were wholly evil, and that without ceasing; the corruption was total (every imagination), exclusive (only evil), and constant (continually); the verse is the strongest statement of human depravity — the heart's thoughts wholly and always evil; the root of sin in the corrupt heart (cf. Jeremiah 17:9; Matthew 15:19).

On the corruption, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the ground of judgment: the total depravity of man's heart is the ground of the coming judgment; the wickedness was not occasional or partial but pervasive and inward; the corruption of the heart issued in the violence that filled the earth (v.11); the flood would purge a world wholly given over to evil. The verse displays the total corruption of man's heart.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wickedness — "God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth," the vast, universal wickedness of mankind (Keil, Ellicott); "every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually," the total, exclusive, constant depravity of man's heart (Jeremiah 17:9, Calvin, Gill); the corruption the ground of judgment (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays man's depravity. The verse marks the wickedness: great. The verse displays the imagination: only evil. The verse displays the heart: corrupt. The verse displays the continuance: continually. The corruption displays man's heart wholly and always evil. The verse displays the wickedness: universal. The verse displays the heart: depraved. The verse displays the evil: continual. The total corruption of man's heart displays the depth of human depravity before the flood: God saw that man's wickedness was great, and every imagination of his heart only evil continually — the heart wholly, exclusively, and constantly corrupt, the ground of the coming judgment. The verse displays the corrupt race, grieving God's heart (v.6).
6:
"And it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart." The witnesses note God's repentance and grief over man — an anthropomorphism. On "it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth," interpreters note the divine repentance. The reformed Calvin and Gill note that "it repented the LORD" is not a change in God's eternal mind or purpose (for God does not change, Numbers 23:19; 1 Samuel 15:29; Malachi 3:6); rather, it is spoken "by an anthropopathy, after the manner of men" — God's "repentance" expresses his real displeasure at man's sin and his changed dealing toward the race (from favor to judgment); God speaks of himself as men do, that we may understand his abhorrence of sin and his resolve to judge.

On "it repented the LORD," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the meaning. Henry and Keil note that God's repenting marks the greatness of man's sin and the change in God's outward dispensation (not in his unchanging counsel); God's "repentance" is his grief and displeasure, leading to the resolve to destroy man (v.7); the language accommodates the divine to human understanding; God truly grieves over and abhors sin, though his eternal purpose stands.

On "it grieved him at his heart," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the divine grief: "it grieved him at his heart" — God is represented as grieved, pained at heart, by man's wickedness; the figure expresses God's holy displeasure and sorrow over sin; not that God suffers as men do, but that sin is truly hateful to him and (humanly speaking) grieves him; the strong anthropomorphism marks how deeply sin offends the holy God. The verse displays God's repentance and grief over man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the repentance — "it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth," God's "repentance" an anthropopathy (not a change in his eternal purpose, Numbers 23:19), expressing his displeasure and changed dealing toward sin (Calvin, Gill, Henry, Keil); "it grieved him at his heart," God grieved and pained by man's sin, the figure of his holy displeasure (Calvin). The verse displays the grieving God. The verse marks the repentance: of the LORD. The verse displays the figure: anthropopathy. The verse displays the grief: at his heart. The verse displays the displeasure: at sin. The language displays God's holy abhorrence of sin in human terms. The verse displays the LORD: repenting. The verse displays the man: grieved over. The verse displays the sin: hateful to God. God's repentance and grief over man display the divine displeasure at sin: it repented the LORD that he had made man, and it grieved him at his heart — an anthropomorphism expressing not a change in God's eternal counsel but his real, holy displeasure and grief over man's wickedness, and his resolve to judge. The verse displays the grieving God, resolving to destroy man (v.7).
7:
"And the LORD said, I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth; both man, and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air; for it repenteth me that I have made them." The witnesses note God's resolve to destroy man and the creatures. On "I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth," the grammatical voices note the sentence. Ellicott and Gill note that "I will destroy (blot out) man whom I have created from the face of the earth" — God resolves to destroy mankind, whom he had made, blotting them from the earth; the corruption (v.5) has reached its term; the Creator will judge his creature; the sentence of universal destruction is pronounced.

On "both man, and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air," the reformed Calvin notes the creatures. Calvin and Gill note that not only man but "beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air" are included in the destruction — the creatures made for man's use perish with him; the animal creation, though innocent, shares in the judgment, since they were made for man and are involved in his ruin; the whole order of the earth is swept away with sinful man.

On "for it repenteth me that I have made them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the repeated repentance: "for it repenteth me that I have made them" — the anthropopathy (v.6) is repeated, marking God's displeasure as the ground of the judgment; God's "repentance" is his resolve to destroy what sin has corrupted; the grief over sin issues in the sentence of destruction. The verse displays God's resolve to destroy man and the creatures.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sentence — "I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth," God resolving to blot out mankind (Ellicott, Gill); "both man, and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air," the creatures perishing with man (Calvin, Gill); "for it repenteth me that I have made them," God's displeasure the ground of the judgment (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the resolved judgment. The verse marks the destruction: of man. The verse displays the creatures: included. The verse displays the repentance: God's. The verse displays the ground: displeasure at sin. The resolve displays God determining to destroy the corrupt world. The verse displays the man: to be destroyed. The verse displays the beasts: with him. The verse displays the judgment: resolved. God's resolve to destroy man and the creatures displays the sentence of the flood: God resolved to blot out man, and the beasts, creeping things, and birds with him, for his displeasure at sin — the whole order of the earth swept away with sinful man, the creatures perishing in his ruin. The verse displays the resolved judgment, against which Noah finds grace (v.8).
8:
"But Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD." The very brief verse notes Noah finding grace amid the judgment. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Ellicott and Henry note that "But Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD" — over against the universal corruption and the resolved destruction (vv.5-7), Noah found grace; the "but" marks the exception; amid a world doomed to judgment, one man found favor with God; the gracious preservation of Noah and, through him, of the race and the line of promise.

On "Noah found grace," the reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Clarke note the grace. Calvin and Clarke note that Noah "found grace (favor)" in God's eyes — not earned by merit, but the free favor of God; Noah's righteousness (v.9) was itself the fruit of grace; God's grace singled out Noah for salvation from the flood; the doctrine of salvation by grace amid universal judgment.

On "in the eyes of the LORD," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the divine favor: "in the eyes of the LORD" — God looked on Noah with favor; the same God who saw the wickedness of man (v.5) saw Noah with grace; Noah is the type of the remnant saved by grace; the one godly man preserved when the world perishes; grace marks the survival of the line of promise. The verse displays Noah finding grace amid the judgment.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "But Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD," Noah the exception, finding favor amid the doomed world (Ellicott, Henry); "Noah found grace," the free favor of God, not earned (Calvin, Clarke); "in the eyes of the LORD," God's favor on Noah, the remnant saved by grace (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the favored Noah. The verse marks the contrast: but Noah. The verse displays the grace: found. The verse displays the favor: God's. The verse displays the salvation: by grace. The grace displays Noah preserved amid the universal judgment. The verse displays the Noah: favored. The verse displays the grace: free. The verse displays the LORD: gracious. Noah finding grace amid the judgment displays the gracious exception: but Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD — amid a world doomed to destruction, one man found God's free favor, the remnant saved by grace, through whom the race and the line of promise would be preserved. The verse displays the favored Noah, the just and perfect man (v.9).
9:
"These are the generations of Noah: Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God." The witnesses note Noah's character — just, perfect, and walking with God. On "These are the generations of Noah," the grammatical voices note the new section. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "These are the generations (toledoth) of Noah" — the formula introduces the account of Noah and the flood (chs. 6-9); the history turns to Noah, the one righteous man, and his preservation.

On "Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations," the reformed Calvin and Gill note Noah's righteousness. Calvin and Gill note that "Noah was a just (righteous) man and perfect (blameless, upright) in his generations" — Noah was righteous (justified by faith, Hebrews 11:7) and blameless in his conduct, "perfect" not as sinless but as upright and sincere, complete in integrity; "in his generations" — amid a corrupt and wicked age, Noah stood out as righteous; his integrity shone against the darkness of his times.

On "Noah walked with God," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the godly walk: "Noah walked with God" — like Enoch (5:24), Noah walked with God in close fellowship, faith, and obedience; his life was a walk of communion with God; the few words paint the godly man amid an ungodly world; Noah's righteousness, integrity, and walk with God are the fruit of the grace he found (v.8), and the ground of his preservation. The verse displays Noah's character — just, perfect, and walking with God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the character — "Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations," Noah righteous (by faith) and blameless amid a corrupt age (Hebrews 11:7, Calvin, Gill); "perfect in his generations," upright and sincere, complete in integrity, shining against his times (Calvin, Gill); "Noah walked with God," his close fellowship with God, like Enoch (5:24, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the righteous Noah. The verse marks the generations: of Noah. The verse displays the man: just and perfect. The verse displays the integrity: amid corruption. The verse displays the walk: with God. The character displays Noah righteous and walking with God in a wicked age. The verse displays the Noah: just. The verse displays the perfect: blameless. The verse displays the walk: with God. Noah's character — just, perfect, and walking with God — displays the godly man amid the corrupt age: Noah was a just and blameless man in his generations, and walked with God — righteous by faith, upright in integrity, and in close fellowship with God, the fruit of the grace he found and the ground of his preservation. The verse displays the righteous Noah, the father of three sons (v.10).
10:
"And Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth." The very brief verse notes Noah's three sons. The grammatical voices note the family. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth" — Noah's three sons (named, as in 5:32), from whom the whole post-flood race would descend; the family of Noah, who alone would be preserved through the flood; the eight souls (Noah, his wife, his three sons, and their wives, 1 Peter 3:20) saved in the ark.

On "three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth," the witnesses note the heads of the race. Gill notes that Shem, Ham, and Japheth are the three sons through whom the earth would be repeopled (9:18-19; 10:1); the whole human race after the flood springs from them; the line of promise continues especially through Shem.

On the family, the grammatical-historical note the preserved household: Noah's family — himself, his wife, his three sons, and their wives — is the remnant preserved in the ark; God preserves not Noah only but his household; through this one family the race and the line of promise survive the flood; the household of the righteous saved with him. The verse displays Noah's three sons.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sons — "Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth," Noah's three sons, the heads of the post-flood race (5:32; 9:18-19, Keil, Gill); the three through whom the earth was repeopled, the line continuing through Shem (Gill); Noah's household the remnant preserved in the ark (1 Peter 3:20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Noah's sons. The verse marks the sons: three. The verse displays the names: Shem, Ham, Japheth. The verse displays the race: from them. The verse displays the household: preserved. The genealogy displays Noah's three sons, the heads of the post-flood race. The verse displays the Noah: father. The verse displays the sons: named. The verse displays the line: preserved. Noah's three sons display the preserved household: Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth — the three sons from whom the post-flood race would spring, the household of the righteous preserved in the ark, the line of promise continuing through Shem. The verse displays Noah's family, in the corrupt earth (v.11).
11:
"The earth also was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence." The witnesses note the earth corrupt and filled with violence. On "The earth also was corrupt before God," the grammatical voices note the corruption. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the earth also was corrupt before God" — the earth (its inhabitants) was corrupt, depraved, and ruined in God's sight; the corruption was not hidden but "before God," open to his all-seeing eye; the whole earth was morally rotten; the corruption that began with the intermarriages (v.2) had spread to fill the earth.

On "the earth was filled with violence," the puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian Clarke note the violence. Henry and Clarke note that "the earth was filled with violence" — the corruption of the heart (v.5) issued in violence: oppression, injustice, bloodshed, and wrong; the strong oppressed the weak; the mighty men (v.4) filled the earth with their violence; the two great marks of the age were corruption (toward God) and violence (toward man); the breach of both tables of the law.

On "corrupt before God... filled with violence," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the ground of judgment: the earth's corruption and violence are the ground of the flood; the whole earth, given over to depravity and violence, was ripe for judgment; God's longsuffering (the 120 years, v.3) had been exhausted; the corrupt, violent world must be purged. The verse displays the earth corrupt and filled with violence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the corruption — "the earth also was corrupt before God," the earth morally rotten in God's sight (Keil, Ellicott); "the earth was filled with violence," the corruption issuing in violence, oppression, and bloodshed (Henry, Clarke); the corruption and violence the ground of judgment (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the corrupt earth. The verse marks the corruption: before God. The verse displays the violence: filling the earth. The verse displays the sin: toward God and man. The verse displays the judgment: ripe. The state displays the earth corrupt and violent, ripe for the flood. The verse displays the earth: corrupt. The verse displays the violence: rife. The verse displays the judgment: due. The earth corrupt and filled with violence displays the ground of the flood: the earth was corrupt before God and filled with violence — the corruption toward God and the violence toward man, the two marks of the depraved age, ripe for the judgment that would purge it. The verse displays the corrupt earth, beheld by God (v.12).
12:
"And God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt; for all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth." The brief verse notes God beholding the universal corruption of all flesh. The grammatical voices note the divine inspection. Ellicott and Henry note that "God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt" — God surveyed the earth and saw it corrupt; the "behold" marks the certainty and the gravity of what God saw; God's inspection confirms the corruption; the all-seeing God beholds the depravity that fills the earth, and judges righteously on the basis of what he sees.

On "all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the universal corruption. Henry and Gill note that "all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth" — all mankind (all flesh, save Noah and his house) had corrupted their way; the corruption was universal — every man had departed from the right way; "corrupted his way" marks the perversion of conduct, the whole manner of life depraved; the universal apostasy and depravity of the race.

On "all flesh," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the extent: "all flesh" — the corruption embraced the whole race (and, in their ruin, the creatures); none was exempt save the household of Noah; the universal extent of the corruption is the ground of the universal judgment; the whole earth, save the ark, was given over to sin. The verse displays God beholding the universal corruption of all flesh.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inspection — "God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt," God surveying and confirming the corruption (Ellicott, Henry); "all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth," the universal corruption, every man's way perverted (Henry, Gill); "all flesh," the whole race corrupt save Noah's house, the ground of universal judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the beheld corruption. The verse marks the looking: God beheld. The verse displays the corruption: confirmed. The verse displays the flesh: all corrupt. The verse displays the way: perverted. The inspection displays God beholding the universal depravity. The verse displays the earth: corrupt. The verse displays the flesh: all. The verse displays the way: corrupted. God beholding the universal corruption of all flesh displays the divine confirmation of the judgment: God looked, and the earth was corrupt, for all flesh had corrupted their way — the universal depravity beheld by God, every man's way perverted, the ground of the universal judgment of the flood. The verse displays the corrupt earth, sentenced to destruction (v.13).
13:
"And God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me; for the earth is filled with violence through them; and, behold, I will destroy them with the earth." The witnesses note God revealing to Noah the coming destruction of all flesh. On "God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me," the grammatical voices note the revelation. Ellicott and Gill note that "God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me" — God reveals to Noah his purpose: the end (the destruction) of all flesh is determined before him; God confides his counsel to the righteous Noah (cf. Amos 3:7); the doom of the corrupt race is announced to the one man who found grace.

On "for the earth is filled with violence through them," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the ground. Henry and Calvin note that "the earth is filled with violence through them" — the violence of mankind (v.11) is the ground of the judgment; the corruption and violence that fill the earth call for its destruction; God's judgment is just, answering to the universal wickedness.

On "I will destroy them with the earth," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the sentence: "I will destroy them with the earth" — God will destroy mankind together with the earth (its surface, the world they corrupted); the flood would sweep away both the corrupt race and the world they had defiled; the universal destruction announced to Noah, who is then commanded to build the ark (v.14) for his preservation. The verse displays God revealing to Noah the coming destruction of all flesh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the revelation — "God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me," God revealing his purpose of judgment to the righteous Noah (Amos 3:7, Ellicott, Gill); "for the earth is filled with violence through them," the violence the ground of the judgment (v.11, Henry, Calvin); "I will destroy them with the earth," the destruction of the race and the world they corrupted (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the announced judgment. The verse marks the end: of all flesh. The verse displays the ground: violence. The verse displays the destruction: with the earth. The verse displays the revelation: to Noah. The word displays God announcing the flood to Noah. The verse displays the end: come. The verse displays the violence: judged. The verse displays the destruction: resolved. God revealing to Noah the coming destruction of all flesh displays the announcement of the flood: God told Noah the end of all flesh was come, for the earth was filled with violence, and he would destroy them with the earth — the doom of the corrupt race revealed to the righteous Noah, who is then to build the ark. The verse displays the announced judgment, with the command to build the ark (v.14).
14:
"Make thee an ark of gopher wood; rooms shalt thou make in the ark, and shalt pitch it within and without with pitch." The witnesses note the command to build the ark. On "Make thee an ark of gopher wood," the grammatical voices note the ark. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Make thee an ark of gopher wood" — God commands Noah to build an ark (a chest or vessel) of gopher wood (a resinous, durable timber, perhaps cypress); the ark is the means of Noah's preservation; God provides the way of salvation from the judgment he announces.

On "rooms shalt thou make in the ark," the witnesses note the chambers. Ellicott and Gill note that "rooms (nests, cells) shalt thou make in the ark" — the ark was to have many compartments or chambers, to house Noah's family, the animals, and the provisions; the careful, divinely-given design of the ark; God instructs Noah in the building.

On "shalt pitch it within and without with pitch," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the sealing: "shalt pitch it within and without with pitch (kopher)" — the ark was to be sealed with pitch (bitumen), inside and out, to make it watertight; the wordplay (kopher, pitch, and kaphar, to cover/atone) has led some to see the ark, sealed and saving, as a type of Christ and salvation (the covering that keeps out the waters of judgment); the ark, well-built and sealed, would bear Noah safely through the flood. The verse displays the command to build the ark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ark — "Make thee an ark of gopher wood," God commanding Noah to build the ark of durable wood, the means of his preservation (Keil, Gill); "rooms shalt thou make in the ark," the compartments for family, animals, and provisions (Ellicott, Gill); "shalt pitch it within and without with pitch," the ark sealed watertight, the saving vessel (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanded ark. The verse marks the ark: of gopher wood. The verse displays the rooms: many. The verse displays the pitch: within and without. The verse displays the salvation: provided. The command displays God instructing Noah to build the saving ark. The verse displays the ark: commanded. The verse displays the wood: gopher. The verse displays the pitch: sealing. The command to build the ark displays God's provision of salvation: Make an ark of gopher wood, with rooms, pitched within and without — the saving vessel divinely designed and sealed, the means of Noah's preservation through the flood. The verse displays the commanded ark, with its dimensions (v.15).
15:
"And this is the fashion which thou shalt make it of: The length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, the breadth of it fifty cubits, and the height of it thirty cubits." The witnesses note the dimensions of the ark. On "this is the fashion which thou shalt make it of," the grammatical voices note the design. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "this is the fashion (pattern) which thou shalt make it of" — God gives Noah the exact dimensions and design of the ark; the building is by divine instruction, not human invention; the ark is made according to God's pattern.

On "The length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, the breadth fifty cubits, the height thirty cubits," the witnesses note the great size. Clarke and Gill note the dimensions: three hundred cubits long, fifty broad, thirty high (about 450 by 75 by 45 feet, taking the cubit at 18 inches) — an immense vessel, with a vast capacity; the proportions (long, relatively narrow, and of moderate height) suited for stability and floating (not for sailing); the great size was needed to house the family, the animals of every kind, and the provisions.

On the dimensions, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the capacity: the ark's huge dimensions gave it ample room for the pairs of all the kinds of animals and their food; the calculation of its capacity (by various reckonings) shows it sufficient for the purpose; the divinely-given design was fit for its task; God's wisdom in the proportions of the ark. The verse displays the dimensions of the ark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the design — "this is the fashion which thou shalt make it of," the ark built by divine instruction (Keil, Ellicott); "three hundred cubits... fifty... thirty," the immense dimensions (about 450 by 75 by 45 feet), suited for stability and capacity (Clarke, Gill); the great size sufficient for the animals and provisions (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ark's measure. The verse marks the fashion: God's pattern. The verse displays the length: 300 cubits. The verse displays the breadth: 50 cubits. The verse displays the height: 30 cubits. The dimensions display the great ark designed by God. The verse displays the ark: measured. The verse displays the size: immense. The verse displays the capacity: ample. The dimensions of the ark display the divinely-given design: the ark was to be three hundred cubits long, fifty broad, and thirty high — an immense vessel of ample capacity, built by God's pattern, fit to house Noah's family, the animals, and the provisions. The verse displays the great ark, with its window and door (v.16).
16:
"A window shalt thou make to the ark, and in a cubit shalt thou finish it above; and the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof; with lower, second, and third stories shalt thou make it." The witnesses note the window, door, and three stories of the ark. On "A window shalt thou make to the ark," the grammatical voices note the window. Ellicott and Gill note that "A window (an opening for light) shalt thou make to the ark, and in a cubit shalt thou finish it above" — the ark was to have a window or opening for light and air; "in a cubit shalt thou finish it above" is variously understood (the window finished to within a cubit of the top, or the roof sloping to a cubit); the ark was provided with light from above.

On "the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof," the witnesses note the door. Gill and Clarke note that "the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof" — a single door in the side, by which Noah, his family, and the animals would enter; the one door of entrance (which God himself would shut, 7:16); the single door (some see a type of Christ, the one door of salvation, John 10:9).

On "with lower, second, and third stories shalt thou make it," the grammatical-historical note the three decks: "with lower, second, and third stories" — the ark was built with three stories or decks, providing ample, ordered space for the family, the animals, and the provisions; the three levels gave room and arrangement for all that must be preserved; the careful, divinely-given structure of the ark. The verse displays the window, door, and three stories of the ark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the window — "A window shalt thou make to the ark... in a cubit shalt thou finish it above," the opening for light and air (Ellicott, Gill); "the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof," the single door of entrance, shut by God (7:16; John 10:9, Gill, Clarke); "lower, second, and third stories," the three decks giving ordered space (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ark's structure. The verse marks the window: for light. The verse displays the door: in the side. The verse displays the stories: three. The verse displays the design: ordered. The structure displays the ark fitted with light, entrance, and three decks. The verse displays the window: made. The verse displays the door: set. The verse displays the stories: built. The window, door, and three stories of the ark display the divinely-given structure: a window for light, a door in the side, and three stories — the ark fitted with light, a single door of entrance, and three ordered decks for the family, animals, and provisions. The verse displays the structured ark, before the announcement of the flood (v.17).
17:
"And, behold, I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth, to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven; and every thing that is in the earth shall die." The witnesses note God's announcement of the flood to destroy all flesh. On "I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth," the grammatical voices note the emphatic declaration. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I, even I, do bring a flood (mabbul) of waters upon the earth" — God emphatically declares that he himself will bring the flood; the doubled "I, even I" marks the divine agency and certainty; the flood is God's own act of judgment, not a natural accident; God brings the waters to destroy.

On "to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the universal destruction. Clarke and Gill note that the flood would "destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven" — all living creatures (men and animals) that have the breath of life would perish; the destruction is universal — all flesh under heaven; the breath of life, given by God, would be taken; the comprehensive sweep of the judgment.

On "every thing that is in the earth shall die," the grammatical-historical note the totality: "every thing that is in the earth shall die" — all that lived on the earth (save those in the ark) would die in the flood; the totality of the destruction; the corrupt world and all its living creatures swept away; the universal death announced as the judgment on a world given over to sin. The verse displays God's announcement of the flood to destroy all flesh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the declaration — "I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth," God emphatically declaring he himself will bring the flood (Keil, Gill); "to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven," the universal destruction of all living creatures (Clarke, Gill); "every thing that is in the earth shall die," the totality of the destruction (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the announced flood. The verse marks the agent: I, even I. The verse displays the flood: of waters. The verse displays the destruction: all flesh. The verse displays the death: every thing. The announcement displays God bringing the flood to destroy all flesh. The verse displays the God: bringing the flood. The verse displays the flesh: destroyed. The verse displays the death: universal. God's announcement of the flood to destroy all flesh displays the divine judgment: I, even I, will bring a flood to destroy all flesh under heaven, and everything in the earth shall die — God's own act of universal judgment, sweeping away all living creatures save those in the ark. The verse displays the announced flood, with the covenant of preservation (v.18).
18:
"But with thee will I establish my covenant; and thou shalt come into the ark, thou, and thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons' wives with thee." The witnesses note God's covenant with Noah and the preservation of his household. On "with thee will I establish my covenant," the grammatical voices note the covenant. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "with thee will I establish my covenant" — God makes a covenant with Noah; the first explicit mention of "covenant" (berith) in Scripture; God binds himself to preserve Noah and his house through the flood (and afterward, the covenant of 9:9-17); the covenant of grace and preservation amid the judgment; God's gracious engagement to save Noah.

On "thou shalt come into the ark," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the way of salvation. Calvin and Gill note that Noah is to "come into the ark" — the ark is the appointed means of his preservation; Noah must enter it by faith and obedience; the covenant is fulfilled through the ark; salvation from the flood is by entering the divinely-provided refuge.

On "thou, and thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons' wives with thee," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the household: the covenant embraces Noah's household — "thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons' wives" — the eight souls (1 Peter 3:20) preserved in the ark; God saves not Noah only but his family; the household of the righteous is preserved with him; the covenant of grace extends to Noah's house, through whom the race would be renewed. The verse displays God's covenant with Noah and the preservation of his household.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the covenant — "with thee will I establish my covenant," God's covenant of preservation with Noah, the first mention of covenant (berith, Keil, Gill); "thou shalt come into the ark," the ark the appointed means of salvation, entered by faith (Calvin, Gill); "thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons' wives," the household, the eight souls preserved (1 Peter 3:20, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covenant. The verse marks the covenant: established. The verse displays the ark: entered. The verse displays the household: preserved. The verse displays the eight: souls. The covenant displays God preserving Noah and his house. The verse displays the covenant: made. The verse displays the ark: the refuge. The verse displays the family: saved. God's covenant with Noah and the preservation of his household display the grace amid the judgment: God established his covenant with Noah, who with his wife, sons, and sons' wives would come into the ark — the covenant of preservation, the eight souls saved through the divinely-provided refuge. The verse displays the covenanted household, gathering the animals (vv.19-20).
19:
"And of every living thing of all flesh, two of every sort shalt thou bring into the ark, to keep them alive with thee; they shall be male and female." The brief verse notes the gathering of the animals, two of every sort, into the ark. The grammatical voices note the preservation of the kinds. Gill and Calvin note that "of every living thing of all flesh, two of every sort shalt thou bring into the ark, to keep them alive with thee" — God commands Noah to bring two of every kind of living creature into the ark, to preserve them alive through the flood; the animals, which would otherwise perish (v.17), are to be preserved in the ark; God's care extends to the preservation of the animal kinds.

On "two of every sort," the witnesses note the pairs. Gill and Clarke note that "two of every sort" — a pair (male and female) of each kind — were to be brought into the ark; the basic preservation of each species by a breeding pair (the clean by sevens, 7:2); the kinds are preserved for the repopulation of the earth after the flood.

On "they shall be male and female," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the breeding pairs: "they shall be male and female" — male and female of each kind, that the species might be propagated and the earth restocked after the flood; God's provision for the continuance of the animal creation; the pairs preserved to renew the kinds; the divine care for the whole creation, not man only. The verse displays the gathering of the animals into the ark.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gathering — "of every living thing of all flesh, two of every sort shalt thou bring into the ark, to keep them alive," God commanding the preservation of the kinds, two of each (Gill, Calvin); "two of every sort," the breeding pairs, the clean by sevens (7:2, Gill, Clarke); "they shall be male and female," male and female to propagate the kinds after the flood (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered animals. The verse marks the creatures: two of every sort. The verse displays the preservation: alive. The verse displays the pairs: male and female. The verse displays the kinds: preserved. The command displays Noah gathering the animals to preserve the kinds. The verse displays the animals: gathered. The verse displays the pairs: brought. The verse displays the kinds: kept alive. The gathering of the animals into the ark displays God's care for the creation: Noah was to bring two of every kind of living thing, male and female, into the ark to keep them alive — the breeding pairs preserved to renew the animal kinds after the flood. The verse displays the gathered kinds, of fowl, cattle, and creeping things (v.20).
20:
"Of fowls after their kind, and of cattle after their kind, of every creeping thing of the earth after his kind, two of every sort shall come unto thee, to keep them alive." The very brief verse notes the kinds of creatures coming to Noah to be preserved. The grammatical voices note the classes. Gill notes that "Of fowls after their kind, and of cattle after their kind, of every creeping thing of the earth after his kind, two of every sort shall come unto thee" — the three classes of land creatures (fowls, cattle, creeping things), each after its kind, would come to Noah, two of every sort, to be preserved; the comprehensive gathering of the animal kinds; each species represented by a pair.

On "after their kind," the witnesses note the species. Gill notes the repeated "after their kind" — each species distinct, gathered by kind; the preservation of the distinct kinds (the species created in ch. 1); the ark would hold representatives of all the land-dwelling, air-breathing creatures, that the kinds might survive.

On "two of every sort shall come unto thee, to keep them alive," the grammatical-historical note the divine gathering: "two of every sort shall come unto thee" — notably, the animals would "come unto" Noah; God himself, by his providence, would bring the creatures to the ark (the animals coming by divine instinct and direction, not all hunted out by Noah); God's hand in gathering the kinds; the creatures preserved alive by God's provision. The verse displays the kinds of creatures coming to Noah to be preserved.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the classes — "Of fowls after their kind, and of cattle after their kind, of every creeping thing... two of every sort shall come unto thee," the three classes of creatures, each after its kind, gathered in pairs (Gill); "after their kind," the distinct species preserved (Gill); "two of every sort shall come unto thee," the animals brought by God's providence to the ark (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered kinds. The verse marks the fowls: after their kind. The verse displays the cattle: and creeping things. The verse displays the pairs: two of every sort. The verse displays the coming: to Noah. The gathering displays the kinds brought to the ark by God's providence. The verse displays the kinds: gathered. The verse displays the pairs: brought. The verse displays the preservation: alive. The kinds of creatures coming to Noah to be preserved display the comprehensive gathering: fowls, cattle, and creeping things, each after its kind, two of every sort, would come to Noah to be kept alive — the animal kinds brought to the ark by God's providence, preserved for the renewal of the earth. The verse displays the gathered creatures, with the provision of food (v.21).
21:
"And take thou unto thee of all food that is eaten, and thou shalt gather it to thee; and it shall be for food for thee, and for them." The very brief verse notes the gathering of food for the ark. The grammatical voices note the provision. Gill notes that "take thou unto thee of all food that is eaten, and thou shalt gather it to thee; and it shall be for food for thee, and for them" — Noah is to gather food of every kind, stored in the ark, to feed himself, his family, and the animals during the flood; the provision of food for the long months in the ark; God's command extends to the practical preservation of life through the flood.

On "of all food that is eaten," the witnesses note the varied provision. Gill notes that Noah was to take "of all food that is eaten" — every kind of food suitable for man and beast; the varied provision needed to sustain the family and the many animals; the gathering and storing of sufficient food for all in the ark.

On "it shall be for food for thee, and for them," the grammatical-historical note the sustenance: "it shall be for food for thee, and for them" — the stored food would sustain both Noah's household and the animals through the months of the flood; God's care provides not only the ark (the refuge) but the food (the sustenance); the complete provision for the preservation of life; Noah's obedience in gathering the food is part of the faith by which he prepared the ark (Hebrews 11:7). The verse displays the gathering of food for the ark.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the provision — "take thou unto thee of all food that is eaten... for food for thee, and for them," Noah gathering food of every kind for family and animals (Gill); "of all food that is eaten," the varied provision needed (Gill); "for food for thee, and for them," the sustenance for all through the flood, God's complete provision (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered food. The verse marks the food: of all kinds. The verse displays the gathering: stored. The verse displays the sustenance: for all. The verse displays the provision: complete. The command displays Noah storing food to sustain the ark. The verse displays the food: gathered. The verse displays the family: fed. The verse displays the animals: sustained. The gathering of food for the ark displays the complete provision for life: Noah was to gather food of every kind, for himself, his family, and the animals — the sustenance stored for the months of the flood, God's care providing both the refuge and the food. The verse displays the provisioned ark, built in full obedience (v.22).
22:
"Thus did Noah; according to all that God commanded him, so did he." The witnesses note Noah's complete obedience. On "Thus did Noah," the grammatical voices note the obedience. Gill and Henry note that "Thus did Noah" — Noah did as God commanded; he set about the great work of building the ark, gathering the animals and food, in obedience to God; Noah's faith showed itself in works (Hebrews 11:7, "by faith Noah... prepared an ark"); his obedience to the seemingly strange command (building a great ship on dry land, against the scorn of the world) was the fruit of his faith.

On "according to all that God commanded him," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the completeness. Calvin and Henry note that Noah did "according to all that God commanded him" — his obedience was complete, exact, and unreserved; he did all that God commanded, not part only; the full, careful obedience of faith; Noah held back nothing, but did everything as God directed; the pattern of thorough obedience.

On "so did he," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the doing: "so did he" — the emphatic repetition stresses that Noah actually did it; his faith was not bare profession but active obedience; he built the ark and made all the preparation over the long years (the 120 years, v.3), enduring the scorn of the wicked; Noah's obedient faith is the model of the believer who, warned of things not seen, prepares for salvation; the righteous man's works flow from his faith. The verse displays Noah's complete obedience.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Thus did Noah," Noah doing as God commanded, his faith shown in works (Hebrews 11:7, Gill, Henry); "according to all that God commanded him," the complete, exact, unreserved obedience (Calvin, Henry); "so did he," the active obedience of faith, the model believer (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the obedient Noah. The verse marks the obedience: thus did Noah. The verse displays the completeness: all God commanded. The verse displays the doing: so did he. The verse displays the faith: shown in works. The obedience displays Noah doing all that God commanded. The verse displays the Noah: obedient. The verse displays the command: fulfilled. The verse displays the faith: active. Noah's complete obedience displays the faith that prepared the ark: thus did Noah, according to all that God commanded him — his full, exact, unreserved obedience, the active faith (Hebrews 11:7) that built the ark and made all preparation, the model of the believer warned of things not seen. The verse closes the chapter, before the entrance into the ark (ch.7).
